title: Whakapapa of the Waterway: Exploring Ngāti Kahungunu Narratives through English | Te Whakapapa o te Awa: Te Tūhura i ngā Kōrero o Ngāti Kahungunu mā te Reo Pākehā
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the pūrākau (traditional stories) and mātauranga (knowledge) of Ngāti Kahungunu connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), which flows near our college. This waterway holds significance as a source of life, a boundary marker, and a repository of ancestral knowledge. The lesson connects to local environmental guardianship (kaitiakitanga) practices.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare a traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a contemporary written text describing the same location, identifying themes of kaitiakitanga.

Compose a written piece (e.g., a poem, short narrative, or persuasive letter) that integrates specific vocabulary and themes from the studied pūrākau to express a personal connection to a local place.
Participate effectively in a group discussion (korero), using evidence from texts to articulate ideas about the importance of preserving local stories and environments.
Create a visual representation (e.g., a story map or illustrated panel) that synthesizes key elements from a pūrākau and explains their significance in both te reo Māori and English.

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa's Story
activities.description: Students listen to a local kaumātua or kaiako share the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They use a graphic organizer to record key characters, events, and the core values (e.g., whanaungatanga, kaitiakitanga) embedded in the story. This is followed by a silent walk to a vantage point of the awa for observation and reflection.

activities.title: Tūhura: Text Comparison and Analysis
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze two texts: a written version of the pūrākau and a recent news article or council report about the health of the Mangakōtukutuku stream. Using a Venn diagram or comparison table, they identify similarities and differences in perspective, purpose, language features, and the portrayal of human interaction with the environment.

activities.title: Waihanga: Creative Writing Response
activities.description: Students choose a local landmark meaningful to them (e.g., a tree, hill, stream). Using the pūrākau as a model, they plan and draft a creative piece that gives a 'voice' to this place. They are encouraged to use descriptive language, metaphor, and integrate at least three thematic words from the pūrākau (e.g., kaitiaki, mauri, whakapapa).

activities.title: Kōrero: Structured Group Discussion
activities.description: Using a talking stick or stone to facilitate turn-taking, groups discuss the guiding question: 'How do stories help us care for our environment?' Students must support their points with specific examples from both the traditional pūrākau and the modern text analyzed in previous activities. The teacher acts as a facilitator, prompting for deeper reasoning.

activities.title: Whakaatu: Creating a Story Panel
activities.description: Students create a visual story panel (digitally or on poster paper) that depicts a key scene from the pūrākau. The panel must include labels or short captions in both te reo Māori and English that explain the significance of the depicted elements (e.g., a taniwha as a kaitiaki) and connect them to the lesson's themes.

resources: A local Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua or representative from Mana Ahurea Department (Hastings District Council) to share pūrākau.
Digital access to Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand for background on Ngāti Kahungunu history and storytelling traditions.
The book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' (by Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated) or specific excerpts provided by the local iwi liaison officer.
Hastings District Council's 'State of the Environment' reports or news articles focusing on the Mangakōtukutuku stream.

tasks: Completed graphic organizer from the 'Whakarongo' activity showing comprehension of the pūrākau.
Final draft of the creative writing piece ('Waihanga' activity) with evidence of editing.
Teacher observation notes and a self-reflection rubric on participation in the 'Kōrero' group discussion.
The completed visual story panel ('Whakaatu' activity) with bilingual captions.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features.
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features.
The Arts: Visual Arts - Understanding the arts in context.
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
